summary: My grandfather came over from Denmark and joined the Northern army during the American Civil War. He was originally named Nelsen, but due to a misunderstanding when he enlisted, the name became Nelson. Denmark is where my family roots began before coming to America.

Lawrence Nelson, who was a chef and of Danish extraction, had been raised in Oregon. Though my family eventually settled in Montana, Oregon was part of our earlier American experience. It’s interesting how my grandfather’s journey started in Denmark and then Oregon before Montana.

My grandfather joined the Northern army during the American Civil War after coming from Denmark. His enlistment was a pivotal moment that led to the family name changing from Nelsen to Nelson. The Northern army was part of the defining conflict that shaped our family’s American story.

The American Civil War was significant for my family because my grandfather fought in it as part of the Northern army. It was during this time that his name was altered due to a misunderstanding. This war marked the beginning of my family’s long history in the United States.

My mother, Bertha Meisenbach, came from a family of German and Luxembourger extraction. Her parents were born in Luxembourg, and she moved from St. Louis to Montana as a young girl. She married my father, and I spent much of my early childhood with relatives on her side before she tragically died giving birth to a child who also did not survive.

My mother’s parents were born in Luxembourg, which is where part of my family heritage comes from. My mother, Bertha Meisenbach, carried that Luxembourger background when she moved from St. Louis to Montana. It’s a part of my family’s European roots that influenced my upbringing.

Montana was where I spent much of my childhood and youth. After my mother died, I lived with relatives near Hysham in Treasure County, a wild part of southern Montana. I went to school in small towns like Hysham and Eden, and later attended Carroll College in Helena. Montana shaped much of my early life, from ranch experiences to education and friendships.

My mother came out to Montana from St. Louis, Missouri, as a young girl before marrying my father. St. Louis was part of her early life before she settled in Montana. It represents the migration path my family took from the Midwest to the West.

After my mother died, I was taken to Hysham, Montana, by relatives who were actually my second cousins. Hysham was the county seat of Treasure County, the smallest county in Montana, and it was a wild place with cowboys and frequent shootings. We lived on a ranch about forty miles out of town in a log cabin with a dirt floor. I went to school there until the third grade and had many memorable experiences growing up in that rugged environment.

Treasure County, Montana, is where Hysham is located, and it was the smallest county in the state when I lived there. It was a rough place with cowboys who carried pistols and frequent shootings on Saturday nights. My family’s ranch was about forty miles from Hysham, and I spent about four years living that frontier life on a 160-acre ranch with my cousins.

After attending school in Hysham, I went to a small county school in Eden, Montana, with only six students. I was the only boy among five girls, and I used to bring mice to school to scare them. Eden was part of my early education and childhood adventures in Montana.

My stepmother was born in Oklahoma, and she married my father after my mother died. She was only nineteen, just eleven years older than me, and had a child from a previous marriage. She was strict but fair and played a significant role in my upbringing when I moved to town to live with them.

Charles Russell was a great friend of my father's and a significant figure in my life. I remember vividly that he died in 1926, the year I finished grade school. He once brought me the first fresh pineapple I had ever seen from Hawaii, which was a rare treat back then. I also recall him and my father making whiskey in our basement and sharing stories about the old days. Knowing Charlie Russell personally was one of the great experiences of my life.

Hawaii came into my life through Charles Russell, who brought me the first fresh pineapple I had ever seen from there. This was back when there were no planes, so he brought it by ship, making it a special treat. Hawaii represented something exotic and different in my Montana childhood.

I attended Carroll College in Helena, Montana, which was then called Mount St. Charles. It was both a high school and college with a small student body. There, I got involved in athletics like basketball and football, and I met important people like my football coach Bill Jones and the priests Father McCormack and Father Riley, who influenced my life greatly. Carroll College was where I started to mature and find my place.

Helena, Montana, is where Carroll College is located, and it’s where I moved to continue my education after living on the ranch. I attended both high school and college there and developed my athletic skills. Helena was a central place in my youth for schooling and personal growth.

Sid Smith was my high school basketball coach and a senior in college at the time. He was tough and athletic but had a gentle side that influenced me deeply. He helped me improve my basketball skills by making me practice dribbling for hours, which gave me balance and confidence. Later, he became my sponsor when I converted to Catholicism. Sid was a great friend and mentor throughout my life.

Bill Jones was the football coach who came from Notre Dame to Carroll College in 1930. He was one of the 'Seven Mules' who blocked for the famous 'Four Horsemen.' When he encouraged me to play football despite my size, he gave me confidence I had never had before. Bill Jones remained a lifelong friend and later became the chief judge in Washington, D.C. I still see him and we often talk about football.

The 'Four Horsemen' were a legendary football backfield from Notre Dame, and Bill Jones, my coach at Carroll College, was one of the 'Seven Mules' who blocked for them. Knowing Bill Jones connected me indirectly to that famous football legacy. It was inspiring to be coached by someone with such a prestigious background.

The 'Seven Mules' were the linemen who blocked for Notre Dame’s 'Four Horsemen,' and Bill Jones, my football coach at Carroll College, was one of them. His experience and confidence helped me develop as a football player despite my small size. The connection to the 'Seven Mules' gave me pride and motivation during my athletic career.

Washington, D.C. is where Bill Jones, my former football coach and lifelong friend, became the chief judge of the courts. He is now the counterpart of Judge Sirica and the head judge in the District of Columbia. We still keep in touch, and he often talks about football and our shared past.

Father McCormack was one of the priests at Carroll College who had a significant influence on me as I grew up. Along with Father Riley, he was very good to me and taught me a lot. Their guidance was important during my formative years at the Catholic school.

Father Riley was the principal of Carroll College during my time there and, along with Father McCormack, played a big role in my development. They were supportive and taught me valuable lessons. Their presence helped shape my experience at the Catholic school.

The Catholic Church became a central part of my life when I decided to convert during my freshman year at Carroll College. It wasn’t forced on me, but encouraged by priests like Father McCormack and Father Riley. Sid Smith, my basketball coach, was my sponsor into the faith. The Church provided structure and community during my youth.

I had an appointment to West Point and was very determined to go, but my poor background in mathematics made it difficult. I took algebra classes to prepare, but I struggled and eventually realized I probably couldn’t make it. This was a disappointment because I was bent on attending the military academy, but it wasn’t meant to be.

The Spokane Diocese is connected to my education because the priest who taught my algebra class and questioned my preparation later became Bishop Topel of that diocese. He asked me about my high school algebra background, which highlighted my academic struggles. This connection shows how religious figures influenced both my education and spiritual life.

Bishop Topel was the priest who taught my algebra class and later became the bishop of the Spokane Diocese. He questioned my readiness for the class, which made me realize I was not prepared for West Point. His role was important in my academic journey and showed the intersection of education and faith in my life.

Father Rooney was mentioned among the priests who had a lot to do with me as I grew up at Carroll College. Although I didn’t elaborate much about him, he was part of the supportive religious community that influenced my youth.
Albert Einstein was not mentioned in my story or experiences, so I have no personal connection or memories related to him.

Butte, Montana, was the location of the zone basketball tournament where my high school team placed third during my senior year. It was a memorable event in my athletic career and a highlight of my time playing basketball at Carroll College. Traveling to Butte and competing was a lot of fun and helped me make many friends.
